topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The attic air hung thick and dusty, smelling of aged paper and forgotten things. Leo had been tasked with clearing out the old storage space, a chore his mother had insisted upon. He nudged aside a heavy, moth-eaten tapestry and his breath hitched. There, stacked neatly on a wooden crate, were folders. Not just any folders, but every single assignment from his entire school career, spanning from kindergarten reports to advanced calculus essays. His hands started to tremble, the paper feeling brittle beneath his touch. He backed up slowly, his eyes darting around the shadowed space, a knot tightening in his chest. What was this? Who had done this? He wanted to snatch them, to run, but his feet felt rooted to the floorboards.

[story 2]

"Mom, what is all this?" Maya whispered, her voice thin in the cavernous hallway. She found the box tucked behind the seldom-used bookshelf. Inside, the contents were overwhelming: meticulously organized stacks of homework, tests, and creative projects, all labeled with her name. A cold prickle traced its way down her spine. She picked up a worksheet from third grade, the ink slightly faded. Her heart began to hammer against her ribs, a frantic rhythm against the silence of the house. Every shadow seemed to shift, and she felt an intense urge to flee, to disappear before the weight of this secret became too much to bear.

[story 3]

I found the collection tucked inside my grandmother's old sewing basket. It was a shocking sight—a veritable archive of my academic life, every graded paper, every completed project. My palms grew slick, and I fumbled the papers, the edges crinkling under my anxious grip. A sudden, sharp intake of breath escaped me. The silence of the room amplified the sound of my own ragged breathing. I looked toward the attic door, a deep, unsettling feeling washing over me, a profound sense of being watched. My muscles tensed, ready to bolt, convinced that whatever this meant, it was something terribly wrong.
